title: Write/edit/notebook_edit: canonicalize() races against fs::write (TOCTOU symlink swap)
description:
Steps to reproduce:
1. 

Expected: 
Actual: 

**Dimension:** Security and Access (TOCTOU)

**Location:** /home/doll/OpenClaudia/src/tools/file/write.rs lines 20-34, 62; /home/doll/OpenClaudia/src/tools/file/edit.rs lines 20-40, 98; /home/doll/OpenClaudia/src/tools/file/notebook.rs lines 77-85, 198.

**Violation:**
Each of write_file, edit_file, notebook_edit performs:
  canonical = std::fs::canonicalize(&p);
  ... later ...
  fs::write(path, content) or fs::write(notebook_path, &pretty).
Between the canonicalize() call and the subsequent fs::write, another process (or another thread in the same agent) can replace 'path' with a symlink. fs::write FOLLOWS symlinks on open. The canonicalized path represents the file-at-the-moment-of-canonicalization, not the file at the moment of write. For an attacker with any write access to an intermediate directory (e.g. /tmp/project/.worktrees/*), the replacement is trivial.

Consequences:
1. The guardrails::check_file_access gate (line 43 in write.rs, line 56 in edit.rs, line 88 in notebook.rs) validates the canonicalized path but the actual write happens on the non-canonicalized path variable — the 'blast radius' check can be bypassed.
2. edit_file.rs lines 69 (fs::read_to_string) and 98 (fs::write) use the string 'path' (line 42) derived from canonicalize, but the underlying inode can have changed between the two calls — meaning the model sees content X, writes Y, but the on-disk file is now Z.

**Mandated Refactor:**
1. Open the file ONCE with OpenOptions::new().read(true).write(true).custom_flags(libc::O_NOFOLLOW) (Unix) / FILE_FLAG_OPEN_REPARSE_POINT (Windows). Read, modify, write using the SAME file handle — no path-based re-open.
2. For write_file (new file case), use .create_new(true).custom_flags(O_NOFOLLOW) to refuse replacing a symlink-to-elsewhere.
3. Move the perimeter check (guardrails::check_file_access) onto the open'ed file's /proc/self/fd/N path (Unix) or pass the canonical path derived from fstat, NOT the user-supplied path.
4. On Windows, use CreateFile with FILE_FLAG_OPEN_REPARSE_POINT and verify no reparse point (symlink/junction).
